Payment of Annual Leave Pay on Termination of Employment Contract A leave year means any period of 12 months commencing on the day on which his employment commenced and an anniversary of such day. If an employee has been employed for a leave year and his employment contract is terminated, irrespective of the reasons of termination, he should be entitled to payment in lieu of any annual leave not yet taken. An FDH is entitled to paid annual leave after serving 12 months with the same employer. The number of paid annual leave days increases progressively from 7 days to 14 days according to his/her length of service. Upon termination of the Standard Employment Contract, an employer should give his/her FDH payment in lieu of any untaken annual leave. QUESTION: What happens to my annual leave entitlement if my employment contract is terminated before I have taken all of my leave?

Answer:

If your employment contract is terminated, you are entitled to payment in lieu of any annual leave not yet taken [1]. The daily rate of the payment will be calculated based on the date of termination of your contract [1]. Additionally, if you have been employed for less than 12 months in a leave year and your contract is terminated (other than for summary dismissal due to serious misconduct), you are entitled to pro-rata annual leave pay [2] [4]. 

This is general information, not legal advice.

(IV) Suspected Cases of Prescribed Occupational Disease Q7 What should an employee do if he suspects he has contracted an occupational disease specified by the Employees’ Compensation Ordinance? A7 If an employee suspects having contracted an occupational disease specified in the Second Schedule of the Employees’ Compensation Ordinance, he should seek medical treatment as soon as possible. He should describe to his treating doctor full details of the illness, as well as the occupation, to facilitate the doctor to make diagnosis. If the treating doctor finds or reasonably believes that the employee’s illness is one of the occupational diseases specified in the Second Schedule of the Ordinance, and believes that such illness was or may have been attributable to an occupation specified in the Schedule in which he was employee at any

[2] (Source: A Concise Guide to the Employees' Compensation Ordinance, p.9)
Before employing an employee in a trade or industry in which an occupational disease is prone to be contracted by employees, the employer may, at his own cost, require the employee to be medically examined by a registered medical practitioner. An employee refusing to undergo the medical examination may forfeit his entitlement to compensation in the event of death or incapacity caused by an occupational disease. An employee may still claim compensation under the Ordinance for a disease outside the Second Schedule if it is certified to be a personal injury by accident arising out of and in the course of employment. If an employee suspects being contracted an occupation disease specified in Second Schedule of the Ordinance, he should seek medical treatment as soon as possible. He should describe to his treating doctor full

[3] (Source: A Concise Guide to the Employees' Compensation Ordinance, p.9)
specified in Second Schedule of the Ordinance, he should seek medical treatment as soon as possible. He should describe to his treating doctor full details of the illness, as well as the occupation, to facilitate the doctor to make diagnosis.

[4] (Source: A Concise Guide to the Employees' Compensation Ordinance, p.8)
An employee suffering incapacity arising from an occupational disease is entitled to receive the same compensation as that payable to an employee injured in an accident arising out of and in the course of employment, if the disease is one due to the nature of any occupation in which he was employed at any time within the prescribed period immediately preceding the incapacity caused. The occupational diseases covered by the Ordinance and the prescribed period are specified in the Second Schedule annexed to the Ordinance (see Appendix 2). If the employee has been employed by more than one employer during the prescribed period in the same or in a similar occupation, all the employers may be responsible for paying compensation, though not necessarily to the same extent.

QUESTION: What should I do if I think I have an occupational disease listed in the Second Schedule of the Ordinance?

Answer:

If you think you have an occupational disease listed in the Second Schedule of the Ordinance, you should seek medical treatment as soon as possible [1], [2], [3]. You should describe to your treating doctor full details of the illness, as well as the occupation, to facilitate the doctor to make a diagnosis [1], [3].

QUESTION: How are vested ORS benefits attributed to an employer's contributions used to offset severance payment or long service payment in Hong Kong?

Answer:

According to [2], vested ORS benefits attributed to an employer's contributions can be used to offset severance payment or long service payment. The benefits are divided into "carved-out benefits" [2(e)] and "remaining benefits" [2(f)]. "Carved-out benefits" can only be used to offset the pre-transition portion of severance payment or long service payment, while "remaining benefits" can be used to offset both pre- and post-transition portions [2]. The amount of vested ORS benefits attributed to an employer's contributions for offsetting severance payment or long service payment is calculated as shown in [2(g)] and [2(h)].

QUESTION: If I'm required by my employer to attend a training course, will those hours be counted towards my minimum wage?

Answer:

According to [2] and [3], if you are directed by your employer to attend a training course, the time spent attending the course is considered hours worked for computing minimum wage, as you are in attendance for the purpose of receiving training [2]. This is illustrated in Example 5 [3], where an employee attends a 3-hour training course at the direction of the employer, and those 3 hours are counted as hours worked for computing minimum wage.

 Enrol employees in an MPF scheme Enrolment deadline Employers in the construction and catering industries may enrol both their regular employees and their casual employees in an Industry Scheme. The arrangement for enrolling regular employees in an Industry Scheme is the same as that for enrolling them in a Master Trust Scheme. The MPF arrangements for casual employees in the construction and catering industries are different from those for regular employees. According to the MPFSO, “casual employees” refers to employees in the construction and catering industries whose employment is on a day-to-day basis or for a fixed period of less than 60 days. The MPFSO states that the “60-day” employment rule and the “418” rule under the Employment Ordinance are not applicable to casual employees. This means that employers should enrol casual employees

[2] (Source: Employers' Handbook on MPF Obligations, p.7)
2 For the definition of regular employees, please refer to the section “Employees must be enrolled in an MPF scheme” on page 12 of this Handbook. Some employers in the construction and catering industries also engage regular employees (i.e. staff who are employed for a fixed period of 60 days or more), such as clerks and accounting staff. If these employers employ casual employees and regular employees at the same time, they may arrange for casual employees to join an Industry Scheme and regular employees to join a Master Trust Scheme. However, such employers may also elect to enrol all their employees, both regular and casual, in an Industry Scheme. This arrangement may help streamline the associated MPF administrative work. 

[3] (Source: Employers' Handbook on MPF Obligations, p.15)
Enrol employees in an MPF scheme upon employment Employees must be enrolled in an MPF scheme Employers should enrol all their regular and casual employees in the MPF scheme that the employers have joined. Who counts as regular employees? Employees who are: ♦ Aged 18 to 64; ♦ Working either full-time or part-time; and ♦ Employed for a continuous period of 60 days or more. Who counts as casual employees? Employees who are: ♦  Employed in the construction industry or catering industry on a daily basis, or for a fixed period of less than 60 days.

[4] (Source: Employers' Handbook on MPF Obligations, p.25)
rule and the “418” rule under the Employment Ordinance are not applicable to casual employees. This means that employers should enrol casual employees in an MPF scheme selected by the employers (be it an Industry Scheme or a Master Trust Scheme) even if they are only employed for one day. Casual employees in the construction and catering industries can open casual employee accounts with each of the two Industry Scheme trustees in advance. The two trustees will each provide the employees with a “casual employee card” . If an employer hires casual employees who already have a casual employee account in the same scheme as the employer, the employer does not need to enrol the employees in that scheme again. This arrangement saves time in processing enrolment forms. The employer only needs to ask for the employees’ “casual employee card” , or the account number/casual employee number

QUESTION: Can an employer enroll both regular and casual employees in an Industry Scheme under the MPF?

Answer:

Yes, an employer can enroll both regular and casual employees in an Industry Scheme under the MPF [1], [2]. In fact, employers in the construction and catering industries may find it helpful to enrol all their employees, both regular and casual, in an Industry Scheme to streamline the associated MPF administrative work [2].

QUESTION: How are severance payments and long service payments calculated and offset by an employee's ORS benefits in Hong Kong?

Answer:

According to [2] and [4], severance payments and long service payments are calculated based on the employee's salary and years of service. The payment is divided into pre-transition and post-transition portions. The pre-transition portion is calculated as $15,000 × 2/3 × number of years [2], while the post-transition portion is calculated as $18,000 × 2/3 × number of years [2]. The total severance payment or long service payment is the sum of the pre-transition and post-transition portions [2]. 

The offsetting arrangement allows the employer to offset the severance payment or long service payment against the employee's ORS benefits [1]. The ORS benefits that can be used for offsetting are the vested benefits attributable to the employer's contributions [4]. The "carved-out benefits" can only be used to offset the pre-transition portion of the severance payment or long service payment [4], while the "remaining benefits" can be used to offset both the pre-transition and post-transition portions [4]. 

However, starting from 1 May 2025, the offsetting arrangement will be abolished [3]. For more details, please refer to Chapter 11 and Appendix 2 of “A Concise Guide to the Employment Ordinance” [3].

QUESTION: How is the minimum wage calculated for a monthly-rated employee who works overtime hours?

Answer:

The minimum wage for a monthly-rated employee who works overtime hours is calculated by multiplying the total number of hours worked in a wage period by the Statutory Minimum Wage (SMW) rate [1]. This includes overtime hours [1]. For example, if an employee works 220 hours in a month, including 4 hours of overtime, the minimum wage would be $8,800 (220 hours x $40 per hour) [1]. The employer must then ensure that the total wages payable to the employee, including overtime pay, are not less than this minimum wage [2].

QUESTION: If my employer deducts money from my wages to pay back an overpayment from a previous month, does that deduction count as part of my wages for the current month when calculating the minimum wage requirement?

Answer:

According to [2], if your employer deducts money from your wages to pay back an overpayment from a previous month, that deducted amount is counted as wages payable in respect of the current month, not the previous month. For example, if your employer deducts $400 from your wages in the current month to recover an over-payment made to you in the previous month, this $400 is counted as wages payable in respect of the current month when determining whether your wages meet the minimum wage requirement [2].
